## Extracting a Reward from Preferences

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition: Bradley-Terry Preference Model"}
\begin{align}
P(\sigma^1 \succ \sigma^2) = \frac{\exp \sum_{t} \hat{R}(s_t^1, a_t^1)}{\exp \sum_t \hat{R}(s_t^1, a_t^1) + \exp \sum_t \hat{R}(s_t^2, a_t^2)}, \qquad (s^i_t, a^i_t) \in \sigma_i
\end{align}
:::

<div style="display: flex; gap: 1em;">
<div style="flex: 1;">
<div style="text-align: center;">**Querying**</div>

1. Learn an *ensemble* $\hat{R}_i$.
2. Query the human over $\sigma^1$, $\sigma^2$ that elicit the most disagreement over $\hat{R}_i$.
3. $\mathcal{D} \leftarrow (\sigma^1, \sigma^2, \mu)$

$\mu$ is a distribution over $\{1, 2\}$

</div>
<div style="flex: 1;">
<div style="text-align: center;">**Optimizing**</div>
\begin{align}
\text{loss}(\hat{r}) = -\sum_\mathcal{D}~\biggl[~&\mu(1) \log \hat{P}[\sigma^1 \succ \sigma^2] 
\\+ &\mu(2) \log \hat{P}[\sigma^2 \succ \sigma^1]\biggr].
\end{align}

$\mu$ can represent absolute and uniform preference, as well as incomparable.
</div>
</div>
